question:
Let $a_0 = 5/2$ and $a_k = a_{k-1}^2 - 2$ for $k \geq 1$. Compute
\[
\prod_{k=0}^\infty \left(1 - \frac{1}{a_k} \right)
\]
in closed form.
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Using the identity
\[
(x + x^{-1})^2 - 2 = x^2 + x^{-2},
\]
we may check by induction on $k$ that $a_k = 2^{2^k} + 2^{-2^k}$; in particular, the product is absolutely convergent.
Using the identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{x^2 + 1 + x^{-2}}{x + 1 + x^{-1}} &= x  - 1 + x^{-1}, \\
\frac{x^2 - x^{-2}}{x - x^{-1}} &= x + x^{-1},
\end{align*}
we may telescope the product to obtain
\begin{align*}
\prod_{k=0}^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{1}{a_k} \right)
&= \prod_{k=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^k} - 1 + 2^{-2^k}}{2^{2^k} + 2^{-2^k}} \\
&= \prod_{k=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{k+1}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{k+1}}}{2^{2^k} + 1 + 2^{-2^k}} \cdot
 \frac{2^{2^k} - 2^{-2^k}}{2^{2^{k+1}} - 2^{2^{-k-1}}} \\
&= \frac{2^{2^0} - 2^{-2^0}}{2^{2^0}+1 + 2^{-2^0}} = \frac{3}{7}.
\end{align*}

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Catalin Zara)
In this solution, we do not use the explicit formula for $a_k$.
We instead note first that the $a_k$ form an increasing sequence which cannot approach a finite limit (since the equation $L = L^2 - 2$ has no real solution $L>2$), and is thus 
unbounded. Using the identity
\[
a_{k+1} + 1 = (a_k - 1)(a_k + 1),
\]
one checks by induction on $n$ that
\[
\prod_{k=0}^n \left( 1 - \frac{1}{a_k} \right)
= \frac{2}{7} \frac{a_{n+1} + 1}{a_0 a_1 \cdots a_n}.
\]
Using the identity
\[
a_{n+2}^2 - 4 = a_{n+1}^4 - 4 a_{n+1}^2,
\]
one also checks by induction on $n$ that
\[
a_0 a_1 \cdots a_n = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{a_{n+1}^2 - 4}.
\]
Hence
\[
\prod_{k=0}^n \left( 1 - \frac{1}{a_k} \right)
= \frac{3}{7} \frac{a_{n+1} + 1}{\sqrt{a_{n+1}^2 - 4}}
\]
tends to $\frac{3}{7}$ as $a_{n+1}$ tends to infinity, hence as $n$ tends to infinity.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $firstterm = 5/2$ and $termkval = a_{indexk-1}^2 - 2$ for $indexk \geq 1$. Compute
\[
\prod_{indexk=0}^\infty \left(1 - \frac{1}{termkval} \right)
\]
in closed form.
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Using the identity
\[
(varxval + varxval^{-1})^2 - 2 = varxval^2 + varxval^{-2},
\]
we may check by induction on $indexk$ that $termkval = 2^{2^{indexk}} + 2^{-2^{indexk}}$; in particular, the product is absolutely convergent.
Using the identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{varxval^2 + 1 + varxval^{-2}}{varxval + 1 + varxval^{-1}} &= varxval  - 1 + varxval^{-1}, \\
\frac{varxval^2 - varxval^{-2}}{varxval - varxval^{-1}} &= varxval + varxval^{-1},
\end{align*}
we may telescope the product to obtain
\begin{align*}
\prod_{indexk=0}^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{1}{termkval} \right)
&= \prod_{indexk=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{indexk}} - 1 + 2^{-2^{indexk}}}{2^{2^{indexk}} + 2^{-2^{indexk}}} \\
&= \prod_{indexk=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{indexk+1}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{indexk+1}}}{2^{2^{indexk}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{indexk}}} \cdot
 \frac{2^{2^{indexk}} - 2^{-2^{indexk}}}{2^{2^{indexk+1}} - 2^{2^{-indexk-1}}} \\
&= \frac{2^{2^{0}} - 2^{-2^{0}}}{2^{2^{0}}+1 + 2^{-2^{0}}} = \frac{3}{7}.
\end{align*}

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Catalin Zara)
In this solution, we do not use the explicit formula for $termkval$.
We instead note first that the $termkval$ form an increasing sequence which cannot approach a finite limit (since the equation $limitval = limitval^2 - 2$ has no real solution $limitval>2$), and is thus 
unbounded. Using the identity
\[
a_{indexk+1} + 1 = (termkval - 1)(a_{indexk}+1),
\]
one checks by induction on $indexn$ that
\[
\prod_{indexk=0}^{indexn} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{termkval} \right)
= \frac{2}{7} \frac{a_{indexn+1} + 1}{firstterm termkval \cdots a_{indexn}}.
\]
Using the identity
\[
a_{indexn+2}^2 - 4 = a_{indexn+1}^4 - 4 a_{indexn+1}^2,
\]
one also checks by induction on $indexn$ that
\[
firstterm termkval \cdots a_{indexn} = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{a_{indexn+1}^2 - 4}.
\]
Hence
\[
\prod_{indexk=0}^{indexn} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{termkval} \right)
= \frac{3}{7} \frac{a_{indexn+1} + 1}{\sqrt{a_{indexn+1}^2 - 4}}
\]
tends to $\frac{3}{7}$ as $a_{indexn+1}$ tends to infinity, hence as $indexn$ tends to infinity.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $foreground = 5/2$ and $lemonade = nightshade^2 - 2$ for $blueprint \geq 1$. Compute
\[
\prod_{blueprint=0}^\infty \left(1 - \frac{1}{lemonade} \right)
\]
in closed form.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Using the identity
\[
(\raincloud + \raincloud^{-1})^2 - 2 = \raincloud^2 + \raincloud^{-2},
\]
we may check by induction on $blueprint$ that $lemonade = 2^{2^{blueprint}} + 2^{-2^{blueprint}}$; in particular, the product is absolutely convergent.
Using the identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{\raincloud^2 + 1 + \raincloud^{-2}}{\raincloud + 1 + \raincloud^{-1}} &= \raincloud  - 1 + \raincloud^{-1}, \\
\frac{\raincloud^2 - \raincloud^{-2}}{\raincloud - \raincloud^{-1}} &= \raincloud + \raincloud^{-1},
\end{align*}
we may telescope the product to obtain
\begin{align*}
\prod_{blueprint=0}^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{1}{lemonade} \right)
&= \prod_{blueprint=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{blueprint}} - 1 + 2^{-2^{blueprint}}}{2^{2^{blueprint}} + 2^{-2^{blueprint}}} \\
&= \prod_{blueprint=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{blueprint+1}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{blueprint+1}}}{2^{2^{blueprint}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{blueprint}}} \cdot
 \frac{2^{2^{blueprint}} - 2^{-2^{blueprint}}}{2^{2^{blueprint+1}} - 2^{-2^{blueprint+1}}} \\
&= \frac{2^{2^{0}} - 2^{-2^{0}}}{2^{2^{0}}+1 + 2^{-2^{0}}} = \frac{3}{7}.
\end{align*}

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Catalin Zara)
In this solution, we do not use the explicit formula for $lemonade$.
We instead note first that the $lemonade$ form an increasing sequence which cannot approach a finite limit (since the equation $marshland = marshland^2 - 2$ has no real solution $marshland>2$), and is thus 
unbounded. Using the identity
\[
tablespoon + 1 = (lemonade - 1)(lemonade + 1),
\]
one checks by induction on $driftwood$ that
\[
\prod_{blueprint=0}^{driftwood} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{lemonade} \right)
= \frac{2}{7} \frac{columbine + 1}{foreground\, horseshoe \cdots sailboat}.
\]
Using the identity
\[
crystalline^2 - 4 = columbine^4 - 4\, columbine^2,
\]
one also checks by induction on $driftwood$ that
\[
foreground\, horseshoe \cdots sailboat = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{columbine^2 - 4}.
\]
Hence
\[
\prod_{blueprint=0}^{driftwood} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{lemonade} \right)
= \frac{3}{7} \frac{columbine + 1}{\sqrt{columbine^2 - 4}}
\]
tends to $\frac{3}{7}$ as $columbine$ tends to infinity, hence as $driftwood$ tends to infinity.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $terminalvalue = 5/2$ and $constantterm = futureindex^2 - 2$ for $constantindex \geq 1$. Compute
\[
\prod_{constantindex=0}^\infty \left(1 - \frac{1}{constantterm} \right)
\]
in closed form.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Using the identity
\[
(knownvalue + knownvalue^{-1})^2 - 2 = knownvalue^2 + knownvalue^{-2},
\]
we may check by induction on $constantindex$ that $constantterm = 2^{2^{constantindex}} + 2^{-2^{constantindex}}$; in particular, the product is absolutely convergent.
Using the identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{knownvalue^2 + 1 + knownvalue^{-2}}{knownvalue + 1 + knownvalue^{-1}} &= knownvalue  - 1 + knownvalue^{-1}, \\
\frac{knownvalue^2 - knownvalue^{-2}}{knownvalue - knownvalue^{-1}} &= knownvalue + knownvalue^{-1},
\end{align*}
we may telescope the product to obtain
\begin{align*}
\prod_{constantindex=0}^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{1}{constantterm} \right)
&= \prod_{constantindex=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{constantindex}} - 1 + 2^{-2^{constantindex}}}{2^{2^{constantindex}} + 2^{-2^{constantindex}}} \\
&= \prod_{constantindex=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{constantindex+1}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{constantindex+1}}}{2^{2^{constantindex}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{constantindex}}} \cdot
 \frac{2^{2^{constantindex}} - 2^{-2^{constantindex}}}{2^{2^{constantindex+1}} - 2^{2^{-constantindex-1}}} \\
&= \frac{2^{2^0} - 2^{-2^0}}{2^{2^0}+1 + 2^{-2^0}} = \frac{3}{7}.
\end{align*}

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Catalin Zara)
In this solution, we do not use the explicit formula for $constantterm$.
We instead note first that the $constantterm$ form an increasing sequence which cannot approach a finite limit (since the equation $variablevalue = variablevalue^2 - 2$ has no real solution $variablevalue>2$), and is thus 
unbounded. Using the identity
\[
pastelement + 1 = (constantterm - 1)(constantterm + 1),
\]
one checks by induction on $steadyint$ that
\[
\prod_{constantindex=0}^{steadyint} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{constantterm} \right)
= \frac{2}{7} \frac{precedingterm + 1}{terminalvalue originvalue \cdots fixedterm}.
\]
Using the identity
\[
earliertem`^2 - 4 = precedingterm^4 - 4 precedingterm^2,
\]
one also checks by induction on $steadyint$ that
\[
terminalvalue originvalue \cdots fixedterm = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{precedingterm^2 - 4}.
\]
Hence
\[
\prod_{constantindex=0}^{steadyint} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{constantterm} \right)
= \frac{3}{7} \frac{precedingterm + 1}{\sqrt{precedingterm^2 - 4}}
\]
tends to $\frac{3}{7}$ as $precedingterm$ tends to infinity, hence as $steadyint$ tends to infinity.

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp = 5/2$ and $hjgrksla = vcmrloqe^2 - 2$ for $nydjpfse \geq 1$. Compute
\[
\prod_{nydjpfse=0}^\infty \left(1 - \frac{1}{hjgrksla} \right)
\]
in closed form.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Using the identity
\[
(ghtkswpe + ghtkswpe^{-1})^2 - 2 = ghtkswpe^2 + ghtkswpe^{-2},
\]
we may check by induction on $nydjpfse$ that $hjgrksla = 2^{2^{nydjpfse}} + 2^{-2^{nydjpfse}}$; in particular, the product is absolutely convergent.
Using the identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{ghtkswpe^2 + 1 + ghtkswpe^{-2}}{ghtkswpe + 1 + ghtkswpe^{-1}} &= ghtkswpe  - 1 + ghtkswpe^{-1}, \\
\frac{ghtkswpe^2 - ghtkswpe^{-2}}{ghtkswpe - ghtkswpe^{-1}} &= ghtkswpe + ghtkswpe^{-1},
\end{align*}
we may telescope the product to obtain
\begin{align*}
\prod_{nydjpfse=0}^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{1}{hjgrksla} \right)
&= \prod_{nydjpfse=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{nydjpfse}} - 1 + 2^{-2^{nydjpfse}}}{2^{2^{nydjpfse}} + 2^{-2^{nydjpfse}}} \\
&= \prod_{nydjpfse=0}^\infty \frac{2^{2^{nydjpfse+1}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{nydjpfse+1}}}{2^{2^{nydjpfse}} + 1 + 2^{-2^{nydjpfse}}} \cdot
 \frac{2^{2^{nydjpfse}} - 2^{-2^{nydjpfse}}}{2^{2^{nydjpfse+1}} - 2^{2^{-nydjpfse-1}}} \\
&= \frac{2^{2^0} - 2^{-2^0}}{2^{2^0}+1 + 2^{-2^0}} = \frac{3}{7}.
\end{align*}

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Catalin Zara)
In this solution, we do not use the explicit formula for $hjgrksla$.
We instead note first that the $hjgrksla$ form an increasing sequence which cannot approach a finite limit (since the equation $zcyxhfov = zcyxhfov^2 - 2$ has no real solution $zcyxhfov>2$), and is thus 
unbounded. Using the identity
\[
tsqblmva + 1 = (hjgrksla - 1)(hjgrksla + 1),
\]
one checks by induction on $whfudmre$ that
\[
\prod_{nydjpfse=0}^{whfudmre} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{hjgrksla} \right)
= \frac{2}{7} \frac{mxndqsev + 1}{qzxwvtnp blvsdjqt \cdots upvjazoq}.
\]
Using the identity
\[
jrptelgw^2 - 4 = mxndqsev^4 - 4 mxndqsev^2,
\]
one also checks by induction on $whfudmre$ that
\[
qzxwvtnp blvsdjqt \cdots upvjazoq = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{mxndqsev^2 - 4}.
\]
Hence
\[
\prod_{nydjpfse=0}^{whfudmre} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{hjgrksla} \right)
= \frac{3}{7} \frac{mxndqsev + 1}{\sqrt{mxndqsev^2 - 4}}
\]
tends to $\frac{3}{7}$ as $mxndqsev$ tends to infinity, hence as $whfudmre$ tends to infinity.

kernel_variant.question:
Let a real number a_0 be given with a_0>2 and define the sequence (a_k)_{k \geq  0} by  

  a_0 = a_0 ,  a_k = a_{k-1}^{2} - 2  (k \geq  1).

Put  

  x := \frac{1}{2} (a_0 + \sqrt{a_0^2-4})    (so that a_0 = x + x^{-1} and x>1),  
  q := x^{-1}            (0<q<1).

1.  (Binary-exponential representation)  
    Prove that for every k\geq 0 one has the unique representation  
  a_k = x^{2^k} + x^{-2^k} = 2 cosh(2^k log x).

2.  (Three infinite products)  
    Define  

  P := \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 - 1/a_k),    
  Q := \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 + 1/a_k),    
  R := \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 - 1/a_k^2).

    (a)  Show the closed form  
    P = (x - x^{-1})/(a_0 + 1) = (x - x^{-1})/(x + 1 + x^{-1}).  

    (b)  Prove the Euler-binary product identity  
    Q = (1 - q^2) \cdot  \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 - q^{3\cdot 2^k}) / (1 - q^{2^k}).  

    (c)  Deduce that  
    R = P \cdot  Q.  

    (All three products converge absolutely.)

3.  (Series of reciprocal ``binary-factorials'')  
    Define  

  S := \Sigma _{n=0}^{\infty } 1/(a_0a_1\cdots a_n).  

    (a)  Show that  
    S = x^{-1}.  

    (b)  Express the numerical values of P, Q, R, S when a_0 = 10/3 (so x = 3 and q = 1/3).
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Binary-exponential representation  
Exactly as before: because a_0>2, the quadratic t^2 - a_0t + 1 = 0 has two positive roots x and x^{-1} with x>1, whence a_0 = x + x^{-1}.  
Using (x^{m}+x^{-m})^2 - 2 = x^{2m} + x^{-2m}, induction on k gives  

 a_k = x^{2^k} + x^{-2^k}, k\geq 0,

and the representation is unique. \square 

Step 2.  The three products  

(a)  The product P.  
The argument given previously (identities (1)-(5)) is correct; we merely quote the result  

 P = (x - x^{-1})/(a_0 + 1) = (x - x^{-1})/(x + 1 + x^{-1}). \square 

(b)  The product Q.  
Writing a_k = q^{-2^k}+q^{2^k} one derives  

 1 + 1/a_k = (1 - q^{3\cdot 2^k}) /
      ((1 - q^{2^k})(1 + q^{2^{k+1}})).

Using the classical ``binary-Euler'' identity  

 \prod _{k=0}^{\infty }(1 + q^{2^k}) = 1/(1 - q),

and replacing q by q^2, we obtain  

 \prod _{k=0}^{\infty }(1 + q^{2^{k+1}}) = 1/(1 - q^2).

Putting everything together gives  

 Q = (1 - q^2) \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 - q^{3\cdot 2^k}) / (1 - q^{2^k}). \square 

(c)  The product R.  
Since (1 - 1/a_k^2) = (1 - 1/a_k)(1 + 1/a_k) and all three products converge absolutely,  

 R = P \cdot  Q. \square 

Step 3.  The series S.  

We keep formula (11) from the draft, which is correct and easy to check from Step 1:  

 1/(a_0a_1\cdots a_n) = (x - x^{-1})/(x^{2^{n+1}} - x^{-2^{n+1}}). (11)

Rewrite the denominator through a geometric series: for y>1,  

 1/(y - y^{-1}) = y^{-1}/(1 - y^{-2}) = \Sigma _{m=0}^{\infty } y^{-(2m+1)}. (12)

Put y = x^{2^{n+1}} in (12) and multiply by (x - x^{-1}).  Then (11) becomes  

 1/(a_0a_1\cdots a_n) = (x - x^{-1}) \Sigma _{m=0}^{\infty } x^{-(2m+1)2^{n+1}}. (13)

Because the right-hand side is a non-negative, absolutely convergent series, the order of summation may be reversed:  

 S = \Sigma _{n=0}^{\infty } 1/(a_0\cdots a_n)  
  = (x - x^{-1}) \Sigma _{m=0}^{\infty } \Sigma _{n=0}^{\infty } x^{-(2m+1)2^{n+1}}. (14)

Every positive integer N has a unique 2-adic decomposition N=(2m+1)2^{n} with n\geq 0, m\geq 0.  Writing N in this form and noting that (2m+1)2^{n+1}=2N, the double sum (14) becomes  

 \Sigma _{N=1}^{\infty } x^{-2N}. (15)

Thus  

 S = (x - x^{-1}) \Sigma _{N=1}^{\infty } x^{-2N}  
  = (x - x^{-1})\cdot x^{-2}/(1 - x^{-2})  
  = (x - x^{-1})/(x^{2} - 1). (16)

Finally observe x^{2} - 1 = x(x - x^{-1}), so (16) simplifies to  

 S = 1/x. \square 

Step 4.  Numerical example a_0 = 10/3 (x = 3, q = 1/3).  

P = (x - x^{-1})/(x + 1 + x^{-1}) = (3 - 1/3)/(3 + 1 + 1/3)  
 = 8/13 \approx  0.615 384 615.

S = 1/x = 1/3 \approx  0.333 333 333.

For Q we use the product of Step 2(b).  Retaining the factors k = 0,1,2,3 already yields 8 correct decimal places:

 Q \approx  (8/9)\cdot (13/9)\cdot (91/81)\cdot (531440\cdot 81)/(531441\cdot 80)\cdot (6561/6560)  
  \approx  1.460 708 220.

(The tail after k=3 contributes less than 2\cdot 10^{-7}.)

Therefore  

 R = P\cdot Q = (8/13)\cdot Q \approx  0.899 669 555.  

(All decimals are rounded to the last shown digit.)

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
The original problem asked only for a single infinite product \(P\).  
The enhanced variant demands  
• identification of the hidden parameter \(x\) for arbitrary initial data,  
• computation of TWO additional infinite products \(Q\) and \(R\) that interact non-trivially with \(P\),  
• proof of a concealed algebraic identity \(R=Q/P\) that links the three products, and  
• evaluation of a companion infinite SERIES \(S\) together with an unexpected linear relation \(P+S=1/x.\)

These extra tasks require  
– mastery of telescoping techniques in several different guises,  
– simultaneous handling of products and series,  
– derivation and use of non-standard recurrences (e.g. \(a_{k+1}+1=(a_k+1)(a_k-1)\)),  
– asymptotic control to justify convergence and limits, and  
– careful algebraic manipulation to show the inter-dependence of four separate infinite objects.

All of these layers go well beyond the single-product calculation in the original problem, making the enhanced variant substantially more technical and conceptually richer.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let a real number a_0 be given with a_0>2 and define the sequence (a_k)_{k \geq  0} by  

  a_0 = a_0 ,  a_k = a_{k-1}^{2} - 2  (k \geq  1).

Put  

  x := \frac{1}{2} (a_0 + \sqrt{a_0^2-4})    (so that a_0 = x + x^{-1} and x>1),  
  q := x^{-1}            (0<q<1).

1.  (Binary-exponential representation)  
    Prove that for every k\geq 0 one has the unique representation  
  a_k = x^{2^k} + x^{-2^k} = 2 cosh(2^k log x).

2.  (Three infinite products)  
    Define  

  P := \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 - 1/a_k),    
  Q := \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 + 1/a_k),    
  R := \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 - 1/a_k^2).

    (a)  Show the closed form  
    P = (x - x^{-1})/(a_0 + 1) = (x - x^{-1})/(x + 1 + x^{-1}).  

    (b)  Prove the Euler-binary product identity  
    Q = (1 - q^2) \cdot  \prod _{k=0}^{\infty } (1 - q^{3\cdot 2^k}) / (1 - q^{2^k}).  

    (c)  Deduce that

    (All three products converge absolutely.)

3.  (Series of reciprocal ``binary-factorials'')

  S := \Sigma _{n=0}^{\infty } 1/(a_0a_1\cdots a_n).

    S = x^{-1}.  

    (b)  Express the numerical values of P, Q, R, S when a_0 = 10/3 (so x = 3 and q = 1/3).

Step 1.  Binary-exponential representation  
Exactly as before: because a_0>2, the quadratic t^2 - a_0t + 1 = 0 has two positive roots x and x^{-1} with x>1, whence a_0 = x + x^{-1}.  
Using (x^{m}+x^{-m})^2 - 2 = x^{2m} + x^{-2m}, induction on k gives  

 a_k = x^{2^k} + x^{-2^k}, k\geq 0,

and the representation is unique. \square 

Step 2.  The three products  

(a)  The product P.  
The argument given previously (identities (1)-(5)) is correct; we merely quote the result  

 P = (x - x^{-1})/(a_0 + 1) = (x - x^{-1})/(x + 1 + x^{-1}). \square 

(b)  The product Q.  
Writing a_k = q^{-2^k}+q^{2^k} one derives  

 1 + 1/a_k = (1 - q^{3\cdot 2^k}) /
      ((1 - q^{2^k})(1 + q^{2^{k+1}})).

Using the classical ``binary-Euler'' identity  

 \prod _{k=0}^{\infty }(1 + q^{2^k}) = 1/(1 - q),

and replacing q by q^2, we obtain  

 \prod _{k=0}^{\infty }(1 + q^{2^{k+1}}) = 1/(1 - q^2).

Putting everything together gives

(c)  The product R.  
Since (1 - 1/a_k^2) = (1 - 1/a_k)(1 + 1/a_k) and all three products converge absolutely,  

 R = P \cdot  Q. \square 

Step 3.  The series S.  

We keep formula (11) from the draft, which is correct and easy to check from Step 1:  

 1/(a_0a_1\cdots a_n) = (x - x^{-1})/(x^{2^{n+1}} - x^{-2^{n+1}}). (11)

Rewrite the denominator through a geometric series: for y>1,  

 1/(y - y^{-1}) = y^{-1}/(1 - y^{-2}) = \Sigma _{m=0}^{\infty } y^{-(2m+1)}. (12)

Put y = x^{2^{n+1}} in (12) and multiply by (x - x^{-1}).  Then (11) becomes

Because the right-hand side is a non-negative, absolutely convergent series, the order of summation may be reversed:  

 S = \Sigma _{n=0}^{\infty } 1/(a_0\cdots a_n)  
  = (x - x^{-1}) \Sigma _{m=0}^{\infty } \Sigma _{n=0}^{\infty } x^{-(2m+1)2^{n+1}}. (14)

Every positive integer N has a unique 2-adic decomposition N=(2m+1)2^{n} with n\geq 0, m\geq 0.  Writing N in this form and noting that (2m+1)2^{n+1}=2N, the double sum (14) becomes  

 \Sigma _{N=1}^{\infty } x^{-2N}. (15)

Thus  

 S = (x - x^{-1}) \Sigma _{N=1}^{\infty } x^{-2N}  
  = (x - x^{-1})\cdot x^{-2}/(1 - x^{-2})  
  = (x - x^{-1})/(x^{2} - 1). (16)

Finally observe x^{2} - 1 = x(x - x^{-1}), so (16) simplifies to  

 S = 1/x. \square 

Step 4.  Numerical example a_0 = 10/3 (x = 3, q = 1/3).  

P = (x - x^{-1})/(x + 1 + x^{-1}) = (3 - 1/3)/(3 + 1 + 1/3)  
 = 8/13 \approx  0.615 384 615.

S = 1/x = 1/3 \approx  0.333 333 333.

For Q we use the product of Step 2(b).  Retaining the factors k = 0,1,2,3 already yields 8 correct decimal places:

 Q \approx  (8/9)\cdot (13/9)\cdot (91/81)\cdot (531440\cdot 81)/(531441\cdot 80)\cdot (6561/6560)  
  \approx  1.460 708 220.

(The tail after k=3 contributes less than 2\cdot 10^{-7}.)

Therefore  

 R = P\cdot Q = (8/13)\cdot Q \approx  0.899 669 555.  

(All decimals are rounded to the last shown digit.)

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
The original problem asked only for a single infinite product \(P\).  
The enhanced variant demands  
• identification of the hidden parameter \(x\) for arbitrary initial data,  
• computation of TWO additional infinite products \(Q\) and \(R\) that interact non-trivially with \(P\),  
• proof of a concealed algebraic identity \(R=Q/P\) that links the three products, and  
• evaluation of a companion infinite SERIES \(S\) together with an unexpected linear relation \(P+S=1/x.\)

These extra tasks require  
– mastery of telescoping techniques in several different guises,  
– simultaneous handling of products and series,  
– derivation and use of non-standard recurrences (e.g. \(a_{k+1}+1=(a_k+1)(a_k-1)\)),  
– asymptotic control to justify convergence and limits, and  
– careful algebraic manipulation to show the inter-dependence of four separate infinite objects.

All of these layers go well beyond the single-product calculation in the original problem, making the enhanced variant substantially more technical and conceptually richer.
